titleStmt.title: Letter from John Moorhead to the Under Secretary of Ireland, 24 July 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: John R. Moorhead

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: Letter from John R. Moorhead (1859-1936) to the under secretary for Ireland requesting a week's leave to visit his son in Dublin who is training with the 4th Battalion of the Royal Irish Rifles. John R. Moorhead served as crown solicitor for Antrim and Belfast in 1916 while Sir Robert Chalmers (1858-1938) was the presiding under secretary for Ireland at this time. Chalmers was sent to Ireland as replacement under-secretary for Sir Matthew Nathan, who had resigned along with Augustine Birrell, his chief secretary, following the outbreak of the Easter Rising in 1916.

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
Counties of Antrim & Belfast Crown Solicitor's Office. 30 Rosemary Street, Belfast. July 24 1916 Dear Sir, I expect to complete the work of
Belfast Assizes by the end of the week, and next week
assuming the assize work and all other urgent
matters are completed, I wish to spend a few days
in Dublin, where my son is with a section of
the 4th Batt. R I R engaged finishing his training
at Dollymount. I have not been absent
from my County for a day over three years
past. I should keep in touch with the office
by telephone from day to day so as to return at
once if anything urgent occurred. I shall be glad to know if I
may have leave of absence for a few days,
not exceeding a week. Your obedient servant John R. Moorhead The Under Secretary The Castle Dublin
